description:
Close the claim-race window between offline agents by optionally pushing the state-branch claim commit to the remote as part of `bl claim`. When enabled, a claim only "counts" once it lands on the remote ref; collisions are resolved at push time via git's native CAS (non-fast-forward rejection) plus the existing resolve_conflict() field-level merge.

## Mechanism

On claim with sync enabled:
1. Write claim locally (current behavior).
2. Push state branch to remote.
3. On reject (non-FF): fetch, run resolve_conflict() to merge task state, check whether `claimed_by` still points at us.
   - If yes: retry push.
   - If no: fail the claim with "agent-X got there first" — local state already correct because resolve_conflict prefers earlier updated_at.
4. On unreachable remote: hard fail with a clear message. Do NOT silently fall back to local-only — that re-opens the gap the flag was set to close.

The CAS loop is mechanical because resolve_conflict is already deterministic and tested.

## Config layering (standard inheritance, off by default)

- State-branch config (`.balls/config.json` on `balls/tasks`) — repo-level default. Maintainers of multi-swarm projects flip it on; the policy ships with the repo.
- Local repo config — per-clone override (e.g. dev offline on a plane).
- Per-invocation flag (`--sync` / `--no-sync`) — final override.

Default off. Solo and offline workflows are the common case; "graceful degradation" cuts toward not requiring network for a core operation.

## No protocol enforcement

This is config inheritance, not a lock. A dev who sets `--no-sync` on a repo that requests synced claims can still break the bargain locally, and that is fine — tools shouldn't fight their users (they'd just set a fake remote anyway). 95% of the value comes from agents following the policy by default; if real abuse appears later, that's when to consider enforcement.

## UX affordance for new clones

First time a clone sees a remote-set policy, surface it once — e.g. `bl prime` prints a line like "this repo requests synced claims (remote default; override with --no-sync or local config)". Mitigates the "surprise: my claims are hitting the network" risk for new devs onboarding to a project. Inspectable, overrideable.

## Out of scope

- File-overlap mutual exclusion between different tasks. That's git's job (merge conflicts at review time); `prime_status.rs` already surfaces overlap_files advisory warnings. Remote-on-claim is task mutual exclusion only.
- Protocol-level enforcement (server-side hooks, mandatory client validation).

## Natural follow-up (separate task)

The same race exists on `bl review` and `bl close` transitions. If this lands and proves out, add a parallel `balls.transitions.require_remote` so the front and back doors close together. File as a follow-up once the claim-side design has settled.

## Implementation notes

- Most plumbing exists: state-branch worktree (`src/git_state.rs`), sync logic, resolve_conflict (`src/resolve.rs`). New surface area is mostly the config flag, the push-retry-resolve loop in the claim path, and the one-time UX line.
- Tests should cover: happy-path push, push reject + resolve + retry, push reject + lost claim (other agent won), unreachable remote with sync required.
title: Optional remote-sync on claim for global claim ordering
